Even though vi shares notes with I, the bass shift and chord quality change can make the loop feel like it’s turning a corner while remaining diatonic. 4) It supports many melodic strategies Melodies can emphasize scale degrees 1, 3, 5 for brightness, or lean on 6 and 4 for a different color, and still fit cleanly. The progression is forgiving: many melodic notes become chord tones or benign non-chord tones over at least one chord. If you want, tell me what key you’re thinking in (or give a concrete example like C–G–Am–F), and we can break down the voice-leading and why certain melodic notes “lock in” so well.
Could you break it down in C major specifically, and show what the smooth voice-leading might look like?

Sure. In C major, I–V–vi–IV is: I: C major = C–E–G V: G major = G–B–D vi: A minor = A–C–E IV: F major = F–A–C A “smooth” voice-leading approach usually keeps common tones and moves other voices by step. One four-voice realization (SATB-style, but just as a conceptual model) could be: Chord 1 (C): - Bass: C - Tenor: G - Alto: E - Soprano: C Chord 2 (G): - Bass: G (leap down/up a 4th/5th from C to G, typical in bass) - Tenor: G (common tone stays) - Alto: D (E steps down to D) - Soprano: B (C steps down to B) Chord 3 (Am): - Bass: A (step from G to A) - Tenor: E (G can move down to E by third; or keep G and re-voice) - Alto: C (D steps down to C) - Soprano: C (B steps up to C) Chord 4 (F): - Bass: F (down a third from A to F) - Tenor: F or A (depending on spacing) - Alto: C (common tone from Am: C stays) - Soprano: A (C steps down/up depending on which C you had) There are many options; the main features you’ll often see: - Common tones: C is common between C and Am and F; E is common between C and Am; G is common between C and G. - Stepwise tendency tones: B in the G chord wants to go to C; F in the F chord often wants to go to E (if returning to C), etc. Another very “song-friendly” close-position loop uses a fixed top note pattern (a “line cliché”) like C–B–C–A in the soprano over C–G–Am–F. That gives you a clear melodic thread while the chords change underneath. If you want, I can write out a few alternative voicings: one that emphasizes common tones, one that emphasizes a descending top-line, and one that emphasizes a descending bass line.
Yes, show three alternatives: common-tone heavy, descending top-line, and descending bass line.

Here are three conceptual four-voice sketches. I’ll keep them readable as chord-by-chord note stacks. (These aren’t the only solutions; they illustrate different priorities.) 1) Common-tone heavy (maximize held notes / minimal motion) Goal: keep as many notes the same as possible between chords, especially in inner voices. C (I):  Bass C | Inner G–E | Top C G (V):  Bass G | Inner G–D | Top B Am (vi): Bass A | Inner E–C | Top C F (IV):  Bass F | Inner A–C | Top C or A A more “held-note” variant leverages C as a pedal-ish upper note for parts of the loop: - Keep C in a top voice for I → vi → IV (works because C is chord tone in C, Am, F). - Move only what must change on V (since C is not a chord tone of G major). So you might conceptualize: - Soprano: C → B → C → C (tiny motion) - Alto: E → D → E → F (stepwise-ish) - Tenor: G → G → A → A (small motion; note A over Am and F is chord tone) - Bass: C → G → A → F (functional root motion) 2) Descending top-line (a clear stepwise melody on top) A classic is C–B–A–A or C–B–A–G depending on voicing and extensions. Example with top line C–B–A–A: C:  Bass C | Inner G–E | Top C G:  Bass G | Inner D–G | Top B Am: Bass A | Inner E–C | Top A (A is chord tone) F:  Bass F | Inner C–F | Top A (A is chord tone) This gives a strong “line” while keeping harmony plain. Another option is C–B–C–A (less strictly descending but very common): C: top C G: top B Am: top C F: top A The “return” to C on vi can feel like a reset before settling to A on IV. 3) Descending bass line (create a scalar or near-scalar bass) The literal roots C–G–A–F aren’t descending. But you can re-interpret inversions to create a stepwise bass like C–B–A–G (or C–B–A–A), while keeping the chord functions. One common descending bass version is: C (I): C in bass (root position) G/B (V6): B in bass (first inversion) Am (vi): A in bass (root position) F/C (IV6/4-ish or IV in second inversion): C in bass (not descending) To keep descending, try: C (I): C in bass G/B (V6): B in bass Am (vi): A in bass F (IV): F in bass (this breaks the stepwise descent at the last move) Or use a “line” that descends for three chords then jumps: C → B → A → F. A more continuous descent for a full four-chord loop can be achieved with passing/neighbor tones in bass rather than strictly chord roots, e.g.: C (I) bass C G (V) bass G, but insert an F# or F natural as a passing tone melodically (non-chord tone) to connect—though that changes the purely diatonic feel. In many pop contexts, “descending bass line” often means “a clear downward tendency” rather than strict stepwise for all four chords. The V6 (G/B) is the main trick: it gives you C → B → A as a very satisfying bass motion. If you want, I can show how adding 7ths (G7, Am7, Fmaj7) changes the voice-leading and why it often sounds even smoother.
Yes, add 7ths and explain how that changes the pull or smoothness.

Adding 7ths increases the number of semitone/step resolutions available, which generally increases perceived smoothness and direction. In C major, a very common set is: Cmaj7 (Imaj7): C–E–G–B G7 (V7): G–B–D–F Am7 (vi7): A–C–E–G Fmaj7 (IVmaj7): F–A–C–E What changes: 1) You get more common tones between adjacent chords - Cmaj7 → G7 shares B (common), and you can retain G in an upper voice as a common tone if you re-voice (since G is in both chords). - G7 → Am7 shares G (common) and often C appears as a resolution target (B→C). - Am7 → Fmaj7 shares A? (not common), shares C? yes, shares E? yes. In fact Am7 (A–C–E–G) and Fmaj7 (F–A–C–E) share A–C–E (three common tones!) if you treat them as sets; only G changes to F. 2) The 7th of the dominant has a strong tendency In G7, the chordal 7th is F, which typically resolves downward by step to E (a chord tone in Cmaj7 or Am7 or Fmaj7). This gives a very “inevitable” sensation: F→E is a classic resolution. 3) The leading tone still does its job B (in G7) still wants to resolve to C. So G7 contains two strong tendency tones: - B → C - F → E When both resolve, the voice-leading feels particularly locked-in. A compact voice-leading example (four voices): Cmaj7: Bass C | Inner G–E | Top B G7:    Bass G | Inner F–D | Top B (keep B common) Am7:   Bass A | Inner G–E | Top C (B→C, F→E) Fmaj7: Bass F | Inner A–E | Top C (G→F or keep G as non-chord tone briefly) Notice how many voices move by step: - F→E - B→C - G→F (if you move that way) Even when the bass leaps, upper voices can move minimally. One subtle point: Cmaj7 has B, which is the leading tone of the key. That can make tonic feel slightly less “final” than plain C, which is why loops with maj7s often feel more continuously flowing and less conclusively resolved. If you want to go deeper, we can analyze the progression with Roman numeral function plus a “tonic expansion” view (T–D–T–PD), or with scale-degree bass and soprano lines.
Do the functional analysis and also explain the “deceptive” aspect of V to vi in this loop.

Functional analysis in C major: - I (C): Tonic (T). Establishes key center; stable. - V (G): Dominant (D). Strong tendency to resolve to tonic; contains leading tone B. - vi (Am): Often treated as tonic-prolongational (T substitute) or as part of a deceptive resolution. - IV (F): Predominant (PD). Prepares dominant; can also act as a plagal color. So one functional shorthand is: T → D → T (via deceptive) → PD → (back to D or T depending on continuation). Deceptive aspect (V → vi): In classical functional terms, V “wants” to resolve to I (authentic cadence). A deceptive cadence occurs when V goes to vi instead. In C major: G (G–B–D) to Am (A–C–E). Why it works: - Voice-leading similarity: In V→I, B usually resolves to C, and D often resolves to E or stays as common tone depending on voicing. In V→vi, B still resolves to C (shared with Am), and D can resolve to E (also in Am). So two core tendency tones resolve as expected, which satisfies the ear’s expectation, but the bass goes to A instead of C. - Shared tones between I and vi: vi contains scale degrees 6–1–3, while I is 1–3–5. Two common tones (1 and 3) mean vi can feel like a “tonic region” despite being minor. In the loop, the deceptive move is especially effective because it avoids a full stop. If the progression were I–V–I–IV, the V→I could sound cadential and potentially too conclusive for a repeating pattern. By going to vi, you get “resolution of tendency tones” without “cadence closure,” which is ideal for looping. Then IV functions as a setup chord. From vi to IV, you often get a gentle shift: Am to F is a downward third relationship, and both are diatonic. IV then points naturally to V (if you extend the loop) or can return to I with a plagal feel.
How does this relate to the “axis progression” idea, and why does it support so many melodies?

“Axis progression” is often used to describe a family of four-chord loops that cycle through the primary triads and their relative minor in a way that feels self-contained and repeatable. I–V–vi–IV is one of the most cited examples. It’s “axis-like” because: - It outlines strong tonal pillars: I and V are the core of tonal gravity, IV is the main predominant, and vi is the relative minor (closely related). - It cycles without demanding a cadence: it contains dominant function but typically avoids a strong authentic cadence landing on I right after V (because it goes to vi). - It is diatonic and symmetric-ish in perceived stability: you have stable (I), unstable (V), stable-ish (vi), and preparatory (IV), then back around. Why it supports many melodies: 1) High chord-tone coverage of the scale Across C, G, Am, F, you cover all seven diatonic scale degrees as chord tones somewhere: - C: C E G - G: G B D - Am: A C E - F: F A C Collectively: A B C D E F G all appear. That means almost any scale-degree a melody hits will be a chord tone at least part of the time, and when it’s not, it’s often a standard non-chord tone (passing, neighbor, suspension) that resolves nicely. 2) Multiple stable “melodic anchors” Scale degrees 1 (C), 3 (E), and 5 (G) are stable over I; 2 (D) and 7 (B) are stable over V; 6 (A) is stable over vi and IV; 4 (F) is stable over IV and can act as a suspension over V. 3) Predictable harmonic rhythm Because the chords repeat with regular timing, melodies can use repetition and sequencing. Even if a melody is simple, the harmonic backdrop gives it a sense of structure. 4) Strong tendency-tone options Over V (or V7), 7→1 and 4→3 (if you include the 7th) are classic resolutions. Melodies can lean into those and sound “directed.” If you want, we can take a short melodic fragment (like scale degrees 5–4–3–2–1) and map it onto the chords to see which notes become chord tones and which become suspensions or passing tones.
Map a few common melodic patterns onto the loop: maybe 5–4–3–2–1, and also 1–2–3–5, and something that emphasizes 6.

In C major, scale degrees are: 1=C, 2=D, 3=E, 4=F, 5=G, 6=A, 7=B. The loop is C | G | Am | F. I’ll assume one melodic note per chord at first (a simplified mapping), then comment on how it behaves if you place the pattern more densely. Pattern A: 5–4–3–2–1 = G–F–E–D–C If you place one note per chord across the four chords, you need four notes, but the pattern has five. So either you extend to five chords (repeat I at the end) or fit multiple notes per chord. Two ways: A1) Across five harmonic positions: C | G | Am | F | C - Over C (I): G (5) is chord tone (C–E–G). Stable. - Over G (V): F (4) is a non-chord tone if the chord is plain G (G–B–D). But it becomes a chord tone if you use G7 (G–B–D–F). Even on plain G, F is a very common 4–3 suspension tendency to E, or it can be treated as a “color” tone in pop usage. - Over Am (vi): E (3) is chord tone (A–C–E). Stable. - Over F (IV): D (2) is non-chord tone (F–A–C). As a 6th above F, it can be a melodic passing tone that wants to go to C or E depending on context; or you can treat the harmony as Fadd6 (F–A–C–D) if you like that sonority. - Over C (I): C (1) is chord tone, stable. A2) Two notes per chord (more realistic): Over C: G–F (5–4) where F is a passing/neighbor to E later Over G: E–D (3–2) (E is non-chord tone over G but can be a 13th; D is chord tone) Over Am: C (1) or E (3) Over F: C (5) or A (3) Then resolve to C on the next I. Pattern B: 1–2–3–5 = C–D–E–G Over C (I): C is chord tone. Over G (V): D is chord tone (G–B–D). Over Am (vi): E is chord tone (A–C–E). Over F (IV): G is non-chord tone (F–A–C). But G is a very common 9th over F; if you interpret it as Fadd9 (F–A–C–G), it’s consonant in pop language. Or it can be a passing tone into the next chord (C), where G becomes chord tone again. This pattern works well because each note is either a chord tone or a very standard extension. Pattern C (emphasize 6): 6–6–5–6 or 6–5–6–3 Let’s do 6–5–6–3 = A–G–A–E Over C (I): A is not a triad chord tone, but it’s the 6th; Cadd6 is a ubiquitous pop sonority. So A can sound “inside” if the harmony is treated as C6 or Cadd6. Over G (V): G is chord tone. Over Am (vi): A is chord tone (root). Over F (IV): E is not a triad chord tone, but it’s the major 7th of F (Fmaj7) or a common upper neighbor that wants to resolve to F or remain as color. A very stable 6-emphasis pattern is 6–5–6–6 = A–G–A–A: - Over C: A (color/6) - Over G: G (chord tone) - Over Am: A (chord tone) - Over F: A (chord tone) That last A over F is especially “locked,” because A is the third of F, so it feels stable. If you tell me whether you’re thinking of triads only or allowing common pop extensions (add9, maj7, 6), I can show how the same melody moves from “non-chord tones” to “stable extensions,” which is a big part of why the loop is flexible.
Assume common pop extensions are allowed. Explain how add9, sus4, and 6 chords interact with this loop without changing its identity.

In pop harmony, “identity” is often anchored by root motion and scale-degree function more than by strict triad quality. You can add extensions that preserve the chord’s root and basic function while enriching the sonority. For C–G–Am–F: 1) add9 - Cadd9: C–E–G–D. Adds scale degree 2, which is diatonic and tends to sound open. It doesn’t change the chord’s function (still tonic). - Gadd9: G–B–D–A. Adds 2 of the chord (scale degree 6 in key). Still dominant-function-ish, though the added A can soften the “dominant bite.” - Amadd9: A–C–E–B. Adds scale degree 7 of the key (B), which can create a gentle pull to C without leaving vi. - Fadd9: F–A–C–G. Adds scale degree 5, often a very consonant color that can connect melodically to G in the next chord or remain as a common tone if the melody is on G. add9 chords keep the triad present; the 9th is a color tone. Since it’s diatonic, it tends to integrate smoothly. 2) sus4 Sus chords replace the third with the fourth, delaying or avoiding the chord’s quality (major/minor) while maintaining the root and dominant/tonic framework. - Csus2 or Csus4 can act as tonic with a suspended quality; the pull is usually toward C major (E as the resolution of sus4’s F). - Gsus4 (G–C–D) is extremely common; it emphasizes the dominant root while putting C (scale degree 1) on top, which can make the dominant feel less “final” and more loop-friendly. Resolving Gsus4 → G (C→B) creates a tiny internal cadence-like motion without changing the progression. - Asus4 is less diatonic if you insist on D as 4 (A–D–E) which is diatonic; it can work, but often Am is left unsuspended. - Fsus2 (F–G–C) or Fsus4 (F–Bb–C; Bb is not diatonic) so in key you more often see Fadd9 (with G) or Fsus2 rather than strict sus4. Sus usage keeps the root motion and harmonic timing identical; it just changes the internal tension and release within each chord. 3) 6 chords - C6: C–E–G–A. Adds scale degree 6; reads as tonic with warmth and continuity (and it shares tones with Am, which helps the I→vi relationship). - Am6: A–C–E–F#. That introduces chromaticism (F#) in C major, so it’s less purely diatonic. More common is Am7 or Amadd9. - F6: F–A–C–D. Adds scale degree 2; also very common as a gentle predominant color. - G6: G–B–D–E. Adds scale degree 3; can feel less dominant and more “mixolydian” color, but still functions as V-ish due to root and leading tone B. Why these don’t change the loop’s identity: - The bass (root) motion remains C → G → A → F (or related inversions). - The functional roles remain: tonic-ish, dominant-ish, tonic/substitute, predominant-ish. - The added tones are mostly diatonic and can often be interpreted as melodic/harmonic embellishments rather than new harmonic goals. If you want, I can show a “maximally embellished but still recognizable” version, like: Cadd9 – Gsus4 – Am7 – Fadd9, and then explain which tones resolve where.
Show that embellished version and the resolution tendencies between the added tones.

Let’s take: Cadd9 → Gsus4 → Am7 → Fadd9 in C major. Chord tones: 1) Cadd9: C–E–G–D 2) Gsus4: G–C–D (often also includes G in multiple voices; sometimes add A as well, but we’ll keep it simple) 3) Am7: A–C–E–G 4) Fadd9: F–A–C–G Now the “added” or color tones and their tendencies: Cadd9 (the added tone is D, the 9th) - D can resolve down to C (9–8) or up to E (9–3) depending on melodic context. - It also serves as a common tone to Gsus4 (since Gsus4 contains D). That’s a big smoothness factor: you can literally hold D across the change. Gsus4 (the “suspension tone” is C replacing B) - In a traditional dominant, B is the third of G and the leading tone of the key; in Gsus4, you have C instead, which removes the leading-tone bite. - The classic resolution is C → B within the G chord (sus4 resolving to major third). In a loop, you can either: a) resolve it before moving to Am7 (Gsus4 → G → Am7), or b) “resolve” it by moving to Am7 where C is a chord tone, essentially reinterpreting the suspension as a common tone rather than a dissonance needing resolution. Am7 (added tone relative to triad is G, the 7th) - As a chordal 7th, G often resolves downward to F if moving to F (which is exactly the next chord). Here, Am7 → Fadd9: G is common to Fadd9, so instead of resolving, it can be retained. That creates a very smooth “no need to move” effect. - If you want a more traditional 7th resolution feeling, you might use Fmaj7 (F–A–C–E) instead; then G would likely resolve to F (7–6 relative to Am, or 9–8 relative to F if you treat it that way). Fadd9 (added tone is G) - G can resolve to E if you return to C (as 9→3 over C), or to F (9→8 over F) if you want a plainer F triad feel. - In the loop back to Cadd9, G is a common tone (both chords have G), and the 9th D in Cadd9 can be approached from G by step (G→A? not; but G→F# not; so more often the melody treats D independently). Key common-tone links across this set: - D: Cadd9 → Gsus4 (common) - C: Gsus4 → Am7 (common) - G: Am7 → Fadd9 (common) - G also common between Fadd9 and Cadd9 (if you keep it) So you can design voice-leading where at least one prominent tone is held at each chord change, which makes the progression feel cohesive and “inevitable” even with added color. If you want, I can propose a single top-line that explicitly traces those common tones (D → C → G → G, for instance) and show how it sits over the chords.
Do that: propose a top-line that highlights common tones, and then another top-line that creates more tension with suspensions.

Top-line 1: Common-tone highlighting Use tones that are shared across adjacent chords as much as possible. Over Cadd9: D (the 9th) Over Gsus4: D (common tone) Over Am7: C (common with Gsus4 and chord tone in Am7) Over Fadd9: C (common tone) So: D – D – C – C. This is extremely stable and “connected.” It’s not very melodic by itself, but it’s a scaffold: you can decorate it with passing tones (D–E–D, etc.) while keeping the anchors. Another common-tone chain is: Cadd9: G Gsus4: G Am7: G Fadd9: G Top-line: G – G – G – G (a literal pedal). This can sound spacious, with harmony shifting under a constant pitch. Top-line 2: More tension using suspensions Here you deliberately choose non-chord tones that want to resolve, often by step, and time the resolutions. Option A: 4–3 and 9–8 style resolutions Over Cadd9: E (3) or D (9) Over Gsus4: C (sus4 tone) then resolve to B (if you briefly imply G major) Over Am7: B (as 9 of Am, tension) resolve to C Over Fadd9: G (9) resolve to A or F depending on taste A concrete tension line across the four chords could be: D – C – B – A - Over Cadd9: D is chord tone (9). - Over Gsus4: C is chord tone (the suspended 4). - Over Am7: B is not a chord tone (it’s the 9); it creates tension that wants to resolve to C. - Over Fadd9: A is chord tone (3). This line is stepwise descending, and the “tension point” is B over Am7. Option B: Emphasize the “avoid note” concept lightly In tonal jazz theory, certain extensions are “avoid notes” over certain chords due to minor second clashes with chord tones. Pop is more permissive, but the idea can still guide tension. Try: E – F – E – F - Over Cadd9: E is chord tone. - Over Gsus4: F is not a chord tone; it’s like a b7 relative to G, implying G7sus4 flavor. That’s a strong dominant color. - Over Am7: E is chord tone. - Over Fadd9: F is root, very stable. The tension comes from F over Gsus4: it implies a dominant 7th that wants to resolve (F→E) which you do on Am7 (E is chord tone), making the line feel purposeful. If you’d like, we can also discuss why the loop is often heard as “circular” rather than cadential, and how melody can either reinforce the cycle or create the illusion of larger phrases across multiple loops.
Discuss that circular feeling. What makes it feel like it can repeat forever, and how do larger phrases get implied?

The “repeat forever” quality comes from a combination of harmonic syntax and avoidance of strong closure points. Why it loops well: 1) Dominant energy without dominant closure The presence of V creates motion and expectation, but V doesn’t resolve to I immediately; it goes to vi. That sidesteps the strongest cadence (V–I) that would otherwise signal an ending. You still get partial resolution of tendency tones (B→C, and if using V7, F→E), so it feels satisfying, but it doesn’t “land” as conclusively. 2) Tonic is present but not emphasized as an endpoint Starting on I establishes the key, but because the progression continues immediately to V, I acts more like a starting platform than a destination. Also, if I is colored (Cadd9, Cmaj7, C6), it can feel less final than plain C major. 3) Predominant IV sets up continuation IV naturally wants to go to V (or sometimes to I). When the loop returns from IV to I, it can feel like a plagal gesture (IV→I) rather than a strong authentic cadence, especially if the melody doesn’t emphasize a cadential 2–1 or 7–1 motion at that moment. Plagal motion is often perceived as “amen-like” or simply “soft resolution,” which supports repeating. 4) Balanced stability/instability distribution Each chord has a distinct role, but none is so unstable that it demands immediate resolution outside the loop. The loop contains its own tension and release cycles at a small scale, which is ideal for repetition. How larger phrases are implied over multiple loops: 1) Melodic phrasing and contour Even with the same four chords repeating, a melody can create an 8-bar or 16-bar phrase by: - building toward a high point (registral climax) - using longer note values toward phrase ends - repeating a motif with variation - aligning melodic cadences with every second or fourth loop 2) Harmonic rhythm and harmonic “weighting” Even if chords change every bar, you can make certain bars feel heavier by: - adding or removing extensions (e.g., plain triads at phrase ends) - using inversions to create a stronger arrival (e.g., return to root-position I at the end of a phrase) - introducing a stronger dominant (G7) only at the end of a larger phrase, then resolving more clearly. 3) Melodic cadences independent of harmonic cadence A melody can cadence (end on scale degree 1 or 3 with a rhythmic stop) while harmony continues. Conversely, the harmony can “cadence” mildly (IV→I) while the melody avoids closure by ending on 2 or 6. 4) Textural and arrangement cues (in an abstract sense) Even without discussing performance specifics, conceptually you can increase density (more notes, more rhythmic activity) and then thin out at phrase boundaries. In purely theoretical terms: more non-chord tones and faster harmonic embellishments mid-phrase; fewer at the end. If you want a concrete example, we can design a two-loop (8-chord) melodic plan where the first loop ends on a non-tonic scale degree (like 2 or 6) and the second loop ends on 1, creating a question–answer structure.
Create that two-loop melodic plan in scale degrees, and show how it cadences over the second loop.

Let each chord get two melodic notes (so 8 chords total across two loops gives 16 notes). Harmony per loop: I | V | vi | IV, then repeat. Goal: - End of loop 1: “open” (non-1, non-3), maybe scale degree 2 (D) or 6 (A). - End of loop 2: “closed” on 1 (C), with a clear stepwise approach (2–1 or 7–1). Plan (scale degrees; each chord gets two notes): Loop 1 I (C): 3–5 (E–G) V (G): 5–4 (G–F)  (F is a color tone; implies V7 flavor) vi (Am): 3–2 (E–D) (D is 4th of Am; tension) IV (F): 2–2 (D–D) (treat as add6 on F; ends open on 2) So loop 1 ends on scale degree 2 (D), which tends to want to resolve to 1 (C), but you don’t give it yet. Loop 2 I (C): 2–1 (D–C) (melodic cadence gesture right away) V (G): 1–7 (C–B) (C over G can function as sus4, then B is chord tone) vi (Am): 1–6 (C–A) (A is stable here) IV (F): 5–1 (G–C) (G is add9, then resolve to C) Now the end of loop 2 lands on 1 (C). The cadential feeling is created by multiple small resolutions: - D→C (2→1) at the start of loop 2 - C→B (a “sus” resolution over V) - and finally G→C (9→5? more like a melodic drop to tonic) at the very end. This plan is intentionally diatonic and pop-extension-friendly. You could make the second-loop cadence stronger by inserting 7→1 (B→C) right at the end, e.g., end with B–C over IV–I, but since the harmony is IV at the end of the loop, you’d need either an implied V before returning to I or place B as a chromatic/leading tone against IV (which is spicier). If you want a stricter tonal cadence aligned with harmony, we could modify the loop’s last chord or add a turnaround (IV–V–I), but that would change the strict four-chord repetition.
How does this progression relate to the circle of fifths? It doesn’t look like a fifths sequence.

It’s not a strict circle-of-fifths sequence (like vi–ii–V–I), but it still borrows some circle-of-fifths logic in pieces. In C major: - I → V is a fifth relation (C to G is down a fifth / up a fourth). That’s part of the circle-of-fifths backbone: tonic to dominant. - IV → I is also a fifth relation (F to C is down a fifth). That’s the plagal side of the same system (subdominant to tonic). The middle moves: - V → vi (G to Am) is not a fifth relation; it’s a deceptive move substituting vi for I. - vi → IV (Am to F) is a third relation (A to F is down a third), which is common in pop and film music because it’s smooth and shares tones (Am and F share A and C). So the loop can be seen as stitching together two strong fifth-relations (I–V and IV–I) with two “color” moves (V–vi deceptive and vi–IV mediant/third-related). This hybrid is part of why it feels both functionally clear and modern/loop-friendly. If you want to connect it more explicitly to circle-of-fifths motion, you can insert passing harmonies: C (I) → Em (iii) → Am (vi) → Dm (ii) → G (V) → C (I) But the four-chord loop is a reduction: it keeps the most recognizable pillars and removes the in-between steps.
Compare I–V–vi–IV with vi–IV–I–V. They’re the same chords; why do they feel different?

Same pitch collection, different starting point and therefore different perceived hierarchy and trajectory. In C major: I–V–vi–IV: C–G–Am–F vi–IV–I–V: Am–F–C–G Differences: 1) Perceived tonic / “home” emphasis - Starting on I strongly asserts C as tonic immediately. - Starting on vi can momentarily suggest A minor as a center (at least locally), even though the overall set is diatonic to C major. The later appearance of C might reframe it back to C major, but the opening color is different. 2) Cadential placement of V relative to I - In I–V–vi–IV, V occurs early and does not resolve to I, which avoids closure. - In vi–IV–I–V, V is last, which creates a strong sense of “to be continued,” because ending on V is inherently unresolved if you loop back to vi. That makes the loop feel like it’s always pushing forward at the seam. 3) Functional flow vi–IV–I–V can be heard as: T-substitute → PD → T → D. That’s almost a full functional sentence ending on dominant, which is a classic looping device. I–V–vi–IV can be heard as: T → D → deceptive T-substitute → PD. It ends on predominant, which feels like it wants to go to dominant, but in the loop it goes back to tonic. That seam (IV→I) is softer than V→vi. 4) Bass-line and melodic implications Different starting points change which scale degrees are emphasized at phrase boundaries. Many melodies are designed to land on stable notes over the first chord of the loop; changing the first chord changes what “stable” means. So even though the chords are a rotation, the functional punctuation changes. Rotation is a big deal in loop-based harmony: the same cycle can feel like “arrive then depart” vs “depart then arrive” depending on where you cut it.
Can you explain this in terms of tonal center ambiguity and “mode mixture” even if the chords are diatonic?

Tonal center ambiguity can arise without any chromatic mode mixture, simply from emphasis, ordering, and melodic focus. 1) Relative major/minor ambiguity C major and A minor share the same diatonic collection. If a progression foregrounds Am (vi) and uses melodic emphasis on A and C, the ear can interpret A as a temporary center. In vi–IV–I–V, beginning on Am encourages that reading. In I–V–vi–IV, beginning on C discourages it. 2) Modal inflection without chromaticism Even diatonic chords can suggest modes depending on which scale degrees are emphasized melodically and harmonically. For example: - Emphasizing scale degree 7 (B) strongly and resolving to C leans “Ionian/major functional.” - Avoiding B or treating it weakly while emphasizing 6 (A) and 4 (F) can evoke a more “mixolydian” or “pentatonic” flavor in melody, even if harmony remains diatonic. 3) “Mode mixture” as a broader perceptual label Strictly, mode mixture refers to borrowing chords from parallel modes (e.g., iv in major). I–V–vi–IV doesn’t require that. But in practice, people sometimes use “mixture” loosely to describe a blend of major/minor affect within one key area. Here, the presence of vi (minor) within a major-key loop can create that blend: not a borrowed chord, but a borrowed “quality” from the relative minor region. 4) Chord-scale associations with extensions Adding certain extensions can tilt the modal feel: - Gadd9 or Gsus4 can reduce the leading-tone emphasis, softening the sense of classical dominant function and making the loop feel more “modal.” - Using C6 rather than Cmaj7 can reduce the prominence of B (leading tone), again shifting the flavor away from strong Ionian resolution. So the ambiguity is less about actual borrowed chords and more about how strongly the progression asserts C as tonic versus allowing A minor to act like a competing center.
How would you reharmonize the loop slightly to make it less common while keeping the same general function?

A few approaches that keep the overall functional arc (tonic → dominant-ish → tonic/substitute → predominant-ish) but add freshness: 1) Secondary dominants (brief chromaticism, same destinations) C → D7 → Am → F Here D7 acts as V/vi (dominant of A). Functionally: T → applied dominant → T-substitute → PD. It keeps the vi arrival but makes the “V” slot more directed. Or: C → G7 → A7 → F A7 is V/ii or V/IV depending on resolution, but here it goes to F (a bit non-classical). More standard would be C → G7 → A7 → Dm → F, but that expands the loop. 2) Substitute predominant Replace IV with ii (or ii7): C → G → Am → Dm This is I–V–vi–ii. ii is a predominant like IV, often even more “classical” in function. 3) Modal interchange on the IV slot Borrow iv from C minor: C → G → Am → Fm Now the last chord is minor iv (borrowed), which strongly colors the loop while keeping “predominant → tonic” pull (Fm → C is a common borrowed plagal motion). 4) Replace V with iii or V/V depending on taste C → Em → Am → F This reduces dominant force, making it more “floaty,” while still moving through related chords. 5) Inversions and pedal points Keep the same chords but use inversions to create a distinct bass line: C → G/B → Am → F/A Now bass is C–B–A–A, which has a clear line and feels less generic. If you tell me whether you want to stay strictly diatonic or allow one borrowed chord, I can suggest a specific four-chord variant and analyze it with the same level of detail.
Stay mostly diatonic but allow one borrowed chord. Suggest two variants and analyze them.

Variant 1: Borrowed iv at the end C → G → Am → Fm Roman numerals in C: I → V → vi → iv (borrowed from C minor) Analysis: - I establishes tonic. - V provides dominant function, but avoids cadencing to I by moving to vi (deceptive). - vi continues tonic-region feel. - iv (Fm) is the borrowed chord: it functions as a predominant with a strong color. The note Ab (♭6 in C) is the main borrowed pitch, and it tends to resolve down to G or up to A depending on voicing; often it gives a poignant pull back to C when the loop repeats (Fm → C is a borrowed plagal motion). Voice-leading highlight: - From Am (A–C–E) to Fm (F–Ab–C): C is common; A can move to Ab by semitone (very striking); E can move to F by step. That’s very smooth and very colored. Variant 2: Borrowed ♭VII as a dominant substitute flavor C → G → Am → Bb Roman numerals in C: I → V → vi → ♭VII (borrowed from C mixolydian or C minor) Analysis: - ♭VII (Bb major) functions like a “backdoor” predominant/dominant substitute. It doesn’t behave like classical IV, but it often moves to I smoothly (Bb → C) via whole-step root motion, and it introduces the pitch Bb (♭7) which weakens leading-tone-driven tonality and gives a modal flavor. Voice-leading: - Am (A–C–E) to Bb (Bb–D–F): not as common-tone rich as to F, but you can connect by step in voices: C→D, E→F, A→Bb. Three upward semitone/whole-step motions create a strong lift. Both variants keep the general loop identity (four chords cycling, similar harmonic rhythm, tonic and dominant present), but the borrowed chord creates a distinctive color at a predictable spot.
For Variant 1 with Fm, explain how melody choices should adapt so it still sounds coherent.

With C–G–Am–Fm, the main new pitch is Ab (♭6 in C). To keep coherence: 1) Treat Ab as a featured color tied to the borrowed chord - If the melody hits Ab, time it over Fm so it’s a chord tone (the third of Fm). That makes it sound intentional rather than like an out-of-key mistake. 2) Prepare or resolve Ab by step Common stepwise contexts: - A → Ab → G (chromatic lower neighbor motion around A/G region) - G → Ab → A (chromatic upper neighbor) - Ab → G (resolving down) or Ab → A (resolving up) depending on desired contour. 3) Use common tones to bridge into the borrowed chord Between Am (A–C–E) and Fm (F–Ab–C), C is common. A melody that sustains C across that change will make the harmonic shift feel smoother. Then you can introduce Ab as a neighboring motion from A or as a chord tone arrival. 4) Be mindful of the key-defining leading tone B The more you emphasize B→C elsewhere, the more the listener hears C as tonic, and the borrowed iv becomes a colorful detour rather than a key change. If you avoid B entirely, the loop can drift toward a more modal or ambiguous sound, which may be fine but is a different aesthetic. A simple coherent melodic approach: - Over C: emphasize E or G or C. - Over G: use B or D to reinforce dominant. - Over Am: use A or C. - Over Fm: use Ab or C, then resolve to G or C as the loop returns. If you want, I can draft a one-loop melody in scale degrees including the chromatic ♭6 and show its resolutions.
Draft that one-loop melody in scale degrees and note names, including Ab, and show where it resolves.

Over one loop (C | G | Am | Fm), let’s use two notes per chord (8 notes total). I’ll mark chromatic tones with accidentals. Harmony: I (C) | V (G) | vi (Am) | iv (Fm) Melody (scale degrees in C, then note names): - Over C: 3 – 2 (E – D) - Over G: 7 – 1 (B – C)  (C over G can be a suspension-like color) - Over Am: 6 – 5 (A – G) - Over Fm: ♭6 – 5 (Ab – G) So the full line: 3–2–7–1–6–5–♭6–5 Notes: E–D–B–C–A–G–Ab–G Resolutions: - B → C is the classic leading-tone resolution over the V chord. - Ab → G is the borrowed-tone resolution at the end: Ab (♭6) resolves down by semitone to G (5), which is diatonic and also a common tone with the upcoming C chord (since G is in C). That makes the loop seam (Fm → C) feel coherent. If you want a stronger “arrival” on the borrowed chord, you could land on Ab at the start of the Fm measure and hold it, then resolve to G right at the loop back to C.
Step back: Is there a way to explain why people perceive this loop as “emotional” without relying on subjective language, more like cognitive expectations?

Yes. You can frame it in terms of expectation, prediction error, and resolution patterns in tonal listening: 1) Strong schema activation (predictable grammar) Listeners enculturated in tonal/pop harmony internalize probabilistic expectations: V often predicts I; IV often predicts V or I; I predicts stability. I–V–vi–IV activates these schemas clearly. 2) Managed prediction error (surprise without disorientation) The deceptive move V→vi violates one high-probability expectation (V→I) but satisfies others (tendency-tone resolutions B→C, and often D→E; plus vi shares tones with I). So the listener experiences a moderate prediction error that is quickly “explained” by the shared structure. In cognitive terms, that’s a sweet spot: novelty with low cost. 3) Continuous tension regulation (no hard closure) Because the loop avoids a strong authentic cadence at the point where it would normally occur, it maintains ongoing forward drive. The brain can keep generating predictions and updating them at each chord, which supports repetition. 4) Multiple stable reference points (tonic and tonic substitute) Having both I and vi as stable-ish centers creates a kind of dual anchoring: the system can partially re-center without leaving the key. This can be perceived as a shift in “interpretive frame” while remaining coherent. 5) Smooth sensory transitions (voice-leading economy) Stepwise motion and common tones reduce perceptual segmentation: the ear hears continuity rather than abrupt change. Continuity makes the small deviations (like the deceptive move) more salient but still integrated. 6) Rich mapping between melody and harmony Because the chord set covers all scale degrees as chord tones somewhere, melodies can be simple yet feel well-supported. The listener perceives strong coherence between layers, which increases the sense that events “belong” together. This explanation stays in the domain of cognitive expectation and structural properties rather than relying on personal affect reports.
Could you connect this to the idea of “harmonic rhythm” and why the same loop can feel different at different tempos or chord-change rates?

Harmonic rhythm is the rate at which chords change. Even with identical chord order, changing harmonic rhythm changes perceived grouping, emphasis, and how much time the ear has to register function. Key points: 1) Faster harmonic rhythm increases “surface motion” If chords change quickly (e.g., every beat), the listener may perceive the progression more as a color cycle than as a functional narrative. The deceptive aspect of V→vi can become less salient because there’s less time to form a strong expectation of V→I. 2) Slower harmonic rhythm increases functional weight If each chord lasts longer (e.g., two bars), the ear has time to register each function strongly. V’s pull becomes more pronounced, so V→vi can feel more like a deliberate evasion of closure. 3) Phrase alignment changes perceived endpoints If the loop aligns with 4-bar or 8-bar phrases, the chord at the phrase boundary gains structural weight. For example: - If the phrase ends on IV (the last chord of I–V–vi–IV), the boundary feels like a soft arrival. - If you rotate to vi–IV–I–V and end on V, the boundary feels like a strong continuation cue. 4) Non-chord tones and suspensions depend on time Suspensions and appoggiaturas require time to register as tension and then resolve. With very fast chord changes, those effects compress; with slower changes, they become clearer. 5) Metric placement matters Even with the same chord durations, shifting the progression so that certain chords fall on strong beats changes perceived hierarchy. A chord on a downbeat tends to feel more structural than one on a weak beat. So the loop’s “character” is partly a function of harmonic rhythm and meter: it’s the same sequence, but the cognitive segmentation and expectation formation differ.
Give an example where the same chord loop is rehung on different metric accents, and explain the effect.

Suppose we have a 4/4 measure grid and four chords per bar (one chord per bar) versus two chords per bar (two beats each), and also a syncopated offset. Case 1: One chord per bar, aligned to downbeats (strongest structural clarity) Bar 1: C Bar 2: G Bar 3: Am Bar 4: F Here each chord gets a full bar and each change happens on a downbeat. The ear assigns maximum structural weight to each chord; the functional story is clear. Case 2: Two chords per bar, changes on beats 1 and 3 (still strong but more kinetic) Bar 1: C (beats 1–2), G (beats 3–4) Bar 2: Am (beats 1–2), F (beats 3–4) Now the loop completes in 2 bars instead of 4. Because changes happen on beat 3 as well, the “mid-bar” chord (G, F) can feel slightly less structural than the beat-1 chords (C, Am), depending on context. This can subtly increase the prominence of I and vi. Case 3: Syncopated offset (changes on beat 2 or the “and” of 2) Imagine each chord lasts one bar, but the change occurs halfway through the bar (e.g., on beat 3) and is tied over: Bar 1: C (beat 1–2), then G begins on beat 3 and sustains Bar 2: Am begins on beat 3, sustains Bar 3: F begins on beat 3, sustains This creates a situation where the “new chord” is not arriving on the strongest metrical position. The ear may perceive the downbeat as belonging to the previous harmony, and the chord changes feel like overlays rather than pillars. Function becomes less “cadence-like” and more “flowing.” Effect summary: - Downbeat-aligned changes: clearer function, stronger sense of harmonic punctuation. - Mid-bar changes: greater momentum; potential re-weighting toward chords on beat 1. - Syncopated changes: reduced sense of harmonic finality; increased continuity.
How would you teach someone to write a melody over I–V–vi–IV that avoids being generic, using only theory tools?

Theory tools to avoid generic outcomes: 1) Choose a specific structural tone plan (scale-degree targets) Decide what scale degree your melody will emphasize at key points (e.g., end of bar 2, end of bar 4). Many generic melodies simply land on obvious chord tones each time. Instead, choose a plan like: - End of I: 2 (a suspension into V) - End of V: 7 resolving to 1 at start of vi - End of vi: 6 (stay on root) - End of IV: 5 or 2 (to avoid full closure) 2) Use controlled non-chord tones with clear categories Plan where you’ll use: - passing tones (stepwise between chord tones) - neighbor tones (upper/lower neighbors) - suspensions (hold a tone from previous chord that becomes dissonant then resolves) - appoggiaturas (accented non-chord tone resolving by step) If you label them consciously, you’ll make stronger, less default choices. 3) Design a motif and transform it Pick a small cell (e.g., 3 notes) and then apply: - transposition (shift up/down scale) - inversion (mirror contour) - rhythmic augmentation/diminution - sequence (repeat starting on different scale degrees) Generic melodies often lack motivic coherence. 4) Exploit chord extensions deliberately Choose a signature color: maybe 9ths as a recurring tone, or 6ths, or a recurring sus4 resolution. If your melody frequently hits 9 over I and IV (D over C, G over F), it will have a distinctive “open” profile. 5) Use register and contour planning (abstractly) Plan one high point per phrase, not on the obvious chord (often people peak on V or I by default). Put the high point on vi or IV, and support it with harmony. 6) Avoid always starting phrases on chord roots If you start every phrase on 1 over I, it can feel predictable. Start on 3 or 5, or even 2 as a suspension. If you want, I can propose a concrete 4-bar melody in scale degrees that uses a motif and includes at least one suspension and one appoggiatura, then annotate each non-chord tone type.
Do it. Provide a 4-bar melody (two notes per chord, one chord per bar), annotate non-chord tones, and keep it diatonic.

Harmony (one chord per bar): Bar 1: C (I) Bar 2: G (V) Bar 3: Am (vi) Bar 4: F (IV) Melody (two notes per bar). I’ll give scale degrees and note names in C major. Bar 1 over C: 2 → 3  (D → E) - D is a non-chord tone over C triad (C–E–G). Here it functions as an accented appoggiatura if it lands on a strong part of the bar and resolves by step to E (a chord tone). If it’s on a weak part, it’s a passing/neighbor; but we’ll label it appoggiatura for a stronger effect. Bar 2 over G: 4 → 3  (F → E) - Over G (G–B–D), F is a non-chord tone. It can be interpreted as a suspension-like tone (a 4–3 resolution relative to the key center) or as implying G7 (where F would be chordal). Since we’re staying diatonic and chord is G, label F as a suspension (prepared from previous bar’s E? not; so it’s more like an accented non-chord tone). Still, F→E is a strong resolution. Bar 3 over Am: 3 → 5 (E → G) - Both are chord tones in Am7? In Am triad, E is chord tone, G is not (Am triad is A–C–E). But G is diatonic and can be treated as a 7th color; if we stick strictly to triad, G is a non-chord tone. To keep it clean, interpret bar 3 harmony as Am7 implicitly, or label G as an accented passing/extension. I’ll label it as a chordal extension (7th) in pop practice. Bar 4 over F: 2 → 1 (D → C) - D is a non-chord tone over F (F–A–C). It’s the 6th; in pop it can be treated as F6 or an accented neighbor/passing tone resolving to C (chord tone). Label it as a accented non-chord tone resolving (6→5 relative to F: D→C). So the scale-degree line is: 2–3 | 4–3 | 3–5 | 2–1 Notes: D–E | F–E | E–G | D–C Motivic aspect: - The “step up then step down” idea appears: D→E then F→E, and later D→C. It’s small but coherent. If you’d like it to be stricter about triads (no implied Am7), we can change bar 3 to E→C (3→1) or E→A (3→6) to keep chord tones only.
Make a version that uses only chord tones of triads, but still has some tension via suspensions across bar lines.

Using only triad chord tones while still getting suspension-like tension is possible by tying a note across the barline so it becomes a non-chord tone momentarily—however that would violate “only chord tones” at the moment it’s held over. If you mean: choose notes that are chord tones of at least one of the adjacent chords, and use ties so they become suspensions, we can do that while keeping the pitch set limited to chord tones of the surrounding harmony. Here’s a version where each pitch is a chord tone of the chord it belongs to, except the tied suspension moment (which is the point of tension): Bar 1 (C): E – G (3 – 5) Bar 2 (G): tie G from bar 1 into bar 2 as a 4th above D? Actually G is root of G, so not tense. Better: tie E into G chord: E is not chord tone of G. That creates suspension. E is chord tone of C, so it’s “legal” as a carryover. So: Bar 1 over C: E (hold) – G Bar 2 over G: (E held as suspension) → D Bar 3 over Am: C – A Bar 4 over F: C – A Let’s write it clearly with scale degrees and note names: Bar 1 (C): 3 (E, tied) → 5 (G) Bar 2 (G): 3 (E, suspension over G) → 2 (D, chord tone of G) Bar 3 (Am): 1 (C) → 6 (A) Bar 4 (F): 5 (C) → 3 (A) Chord-tone status: - Bar 1: E and G are chord tones of C. - Bar 2: E is not a chord tone of G (tension via suspension), resolving to D which is chord tone. - Bar 3: C and A are chord tones of Am. - Bar 4: C and A are chord tones of F. This creates a clear suspension at the bar 1→2 boundary (E over G), then a resolution to D. All other notes are triad chord tones. If you want more suspension moments, you can also tie C from Am into F (it remains chord tone so it’s not tense), or tie A from Am into F (also chord tone). For a second real suspension, you could tie D from G into Am (D is not chord tone of Am), resolving to C or E.
Add that second suspension (D into Am resolving to C), keep two notes per bar, and write the final melody.

Harmony: C | G | Am | F Melody (two notes per bar), with ties creating suspensions: Bar 1 (C): 3 (E, tied) – 5 (G) Bar 2 (G): 3 (E, suspension) → 2 (D, tied) Bar 3 (Am): 2 (D, suspension) → 1 (C) Bar 4 (F): 1 (C) – 3 (A) Note names: Bar 1: E (tie) – G Bar 2: E (held over) – D (tie) Bar 3: D (held over) – C Bar 4: C – A Suspensions/resolutions: - E is prepared as a chord tone on C, becomes a non-chord tone over G, resolves to D (a chord tone on G). - D is prepared as a chord tone on G, becomes a non-chord tone over Am, resolves to C (a chord tone on Am). Everything is diatonic. Most notes are chord tones at least where they are introduced; the tension is created by the tied-over suspensions.
Nice. Last question: If you had to summarize the “why it works” in three technical bullet points, what would they be?

- Clear functional scaffold with controlled evasion: I and V establish tonality strongly, but V resolves deceptively to vi, satisfying key tendency-tone resolutions without producing strong cadential closure, making the cycle repeatable. - High voice-leading efficiency: abundant common tones and stepwise resolutions (especially with 7ths/sus/add tones) create smooth connections between chords and support coherent melodic lines. - Maximal diatonic compatibility: across the four chords, all scale degrees appear as chord tones somewhere, so melodies can emphasize many different notes (including common extensions like 6 and 9) while remaining structurally “inside” the harmony.
